text: Ladies and gentlemen, this video is aimed at trainees and junior surgeons and will show a step-by-step approach to transanal advancement flap repair for perianal fistulas. This popular type of repair was first described by Arthur Elting from Albany, New York, over a century ago. Interestingly, the flap that was originally described by Elting is quite different from the flap many of us perform. He mobilized the rectal mucosa, but did not make lateral incisions before advancing the rectal mucosa caudally. Even though few randomized data are available, several reviews have concluded that the weighted average healing rate of flap repair is about eighty percent. Furthermore, it is the best investigated procedure for fistulas available, and should therefore be considered the gold standard for the treatment of complex perianal fistulas. The present video will not cover patient selection and imaging. If you are interested in these subjects visit the ESCP resource library and watch the video on patient selection and the video on MRI imaging of perianal fistulas.

Let me illustrate the steps of this procedure in a short animation. In this schematic drawing the external anal sphincter is depicted here, the internal sphincter here, Here you can see the rectal mucosa. The dentate line is shown here. Shown here is a complex trans-fingleric fistula only suitable for complex repair, such as transoidal flap repair.

The surgeon begins by coring out the external part of the trans-fingleric fistula. The procedure continues intra-anally with the excision of the internal opening. The remaining fistula is either excised or curetted, creating a large defect. A thick flap is raised over several centimeters, closing the internal opening. The flap is advanced over the internal opening and sutured in place, covering the defect with healthy tissue.

The surgeon begins the core out fistulectomy, using a Lone Star retractor for deeper tracts. A microcautery device, such as the Colorado needle, is employed to remove granulation tissue up to the external sphincter. The cored out fistula is visible, often removed in one piece.

The surgeon performs a wide excision of the internal opening, creating a large elliptical defect to remove all separated tissue. The advancement flap is prepared to attach to healthy tissue at the distal part of the internal opening. A thin strip of healthy mucosa is excised at the proximal end to ensure the flap's tip is strong. The external wound is temporarily packed for hemostasis, which is removed before suturing the internal opening. The excised internal opening and de-epithelialized landing zone are visible, ensuring adequate coverage for closure. The defect from the internal opening is closed using resorbable braided sutures after kerating the intra-sphincteric space.

The surgeon creates an advancement flap approximately five centimeters long, using a harmonic scalpel for precise cutting and hemostasis. The flap is wider at its base than at its tip, and electrocautery is employed to ensure effective hemostasis. The Lone Star retractor, augmented with a disposable plastic retractor, is used to facilitate the procedure, aiming for tension-free coverage below the sutured internal opening.

The surgeon advances the flap over the internal opening and uses two barbed sutures to incrementally fixate the flap from proximal to distal. The flap is carefully positioned to ensure it is well fixed and well perfused, providing good coverage over the defect.

Additional measures worth mentioning are that we operate only patients with anterior fistulas in prone position. Lithotomy is often good enough. We administer single-shot prophylactic antibiotics only. Patients are admitted for one night. We advise against bicycle riding for two to four months after the operation. Finally, before you commence this operation, ask yourself these questions. Is this operation the best option for your patient at this time? Furthermore, are you the best to perform it? Flap repair is a complicated procedure that is unsuitable for unexperienced hands. It is imperative to have enough exposure to gain this experience and you should ideally be aware of the results of your center. I hope you will find this video useful for your daily practice. If you have any questions or additional comments, please do not hesitate to contact me. Thank you for your attention.
